input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a building – a generic civil infrastructure asset – considered within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain. It spans the complete building lifecycle, from initial design through refurbishment to operational use, and is characterized by the combination of static design information (e.g., BIM models) with runtime operational data (e.g., sensor streams) that make it suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides simulation, analysis and optimization services that deliver real‑time insights, predictive analytics and enhanced decision‑making for the building. These services support data‑driven infrastructure management throughout the building’s lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT integrates static BIM geometric models with behavioral and analytical models derived via model‑driven engineering (MDE). Device models for sensors, actuators, and controllers are added to the BIM as semantic extensions. Structured representations are extracted and transformed into deployable artifacts. Runtime data is exchanged through Eclipse Hono (MQTT/AMQP ingestion) and persisted in Eclipse Ditto as digital‑twin instances, providing a unified data store. Visualization of the twin uses Unreal Engine for immersive 3D rendering. Model relationships link geometric entities to their behavioral models and to live data streams, enabling bidirectional synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuating elements are modeled directly within the BIM environment as BCS actuators and controllers. The framework allows building control engineers to define the control logic that drives these actuators, and the generated Digital Twin includes the capability to issue control signals back to the physical actuators. Remote actuation is supported through the real‑time data exchange mechanism, which forwards commands from the twin to the physical control system, respecting the constraints defined in the BIM‑based actuator model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are incorporated into the BIM model as explicit sensor entities linked to their physical locations. The framework captures sensor measurements via a continuous real‑time data exchange channel, synchronizing the incoming sensor data with the corresponding digital sensor representations. Sampling, transmission rates, and precision are dictated by the underlying physical sensor specifications, while the Digital Twin maintains the semantic association between each sensor reading and its BIM‑defined position and type.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by aligning the enriched BIM models (geometric and behavioral) with live data from the physical building through continuous synchronization. Validation occurs as the framework automatically reconciles runtime sensor and actuator data with the corresponding model elements, detecting mismatches that indicate fidelity loss. Quality assurance is achieved by the automated generation of DT artifacts from the verified BIM models and by maintaining up‑to‑date semantic links, thereby supporting ongoing verification of model accuracy during operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes physical building assets with their digital twins. Sensor readings, actuator states and other operational measurements are streamed from the building control system to the DT as live data streams. The exchange operates continuously (i.e., at whatever rate the underlying sensors produce updates) and is triggered by any change in the physical state. Data are ingested through the DT Framework’s standardized extension points, which allow preprocessing (e.g., filtering, unit conversion) and integration with analytics services before being stored in the digital model. Runtime configuration management ensures that data‑quality rules and preprocessing pipelines can be adjusted on‑the‑fly without redeploying the whole system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication enables the digital twin to issue control commands back to physical building systems. Facility managers use the DT interface to adjust building operations; commands are transmitted through the same modular framework via the standardized extension points to the underlying devices. The description does not provide details about specific command types, message formats, validation or authentication mechanisms, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution strategies, or execution confirmations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The solution adopts a modular architecture with defined extension points that supports interoperability across heterogeneous building‑system components. Runtime configuration management facilitates dynamic adaptation of connections without full redeployment. While the document highlights scalability from individual components to whole infrastructure and mentions reliability through low‑overhead updates, it does not detail the underlying network protocols, topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols employed for network communication.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports the full DT lifecycle from initial device modeling in the BIM environment, through model‑driven engineering (MDE) extraction of structured representations, automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts, deployment, and runtime operation with real‑time data exchange and interactive visualization. At the modeling stage sensors, actuators and controllers are defined within BIM; the transformation stage creates an enriched semantic model; the generation stage produces code‑level DT artifacts; the deployment stage instantiates the twin; the operation stage maintains synchronization and enables monitoring. Transition between stages is managed automatically by the MDE‑based transformation pipeline, requiring minimal manual intervention.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development follows a model‑driven approach where building control engineers enrich BIM models, DT engineers add custom services via standardized extension points, and the platform uses MDE principles to derive structured DT representations. Custom plugins are integrated into the modular architecture, allowing incremental evolution of DT functionality. While the document does not detail quality‑assurance, testing, or version‑control mechanisms, it emphasizes automatic artifact generation and extensibility as core evolution practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin provides continuous, real‑time data exchange that keeps the twin synchronised with the physical building, allowing Facility Managers to monitor sensor and actuator states. Interactive visualisation dashboards present the live data in an immersive interface, enabling operators to assess conditions and issue control commands. The framework’s modular architecture permits integration of custom analytics services that can process the data stream to generate predictive or diagnostic insights, which are presented to the user through the visualisation layer to support decision making. Confidence measures for the recommendations are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution is built around BIM, the de‑facto industry standard for AECO data, and requires that all device definitions and control logic be expressed as BIM extensions. It introduces standardized extension points in the twin’s architecture to ensure interoperability of custom services and analytics. While specific standards (e.g., IFC, ISO 19650) are not cited, the framework explicitly targets compatibility with existing AECO‑specific standards and adopts a model‑driven, standards‑aware approach to integration.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

This is strongly supported by the text: 'DTs are... information constructs... for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists specific tools and technologies used to implement the Digital Twin. The abstract mentions that the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'. Since evidence exists but was marked as missing, the score is 1.

Thus, the characteristic is indeed not present.

The document describes the system's 'architecture patterns' and 'component integration'. It explicitly mentions a 'model-driven framework' and a 'layered system architecture' that 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto' and 'Unreal Engine'. Since the source contains relevant information about the overall orchestration and architecture, the extraction 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source text. It accurately describes the integration of static BIM with runtime data, the use of MDE for transformation, the specific tools (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine), and the goal of bidirectional synchronization. Supported by quotes: 'integration of static models with runtime data', 'extract structured representations... automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

The description accurately reflects the framework's ability to model actuators in BIM and facilitate bidirectional control. It captures the role of the control engineer and the mechanism for sending signals back to the physical system. Quotes: 'enable building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS', 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets'.

The extraction correctly identifies that sensors are modeled in BIM and synchronized via real-time data exchange. However, it includes unsupported technical details regarding 'Sampling, transmission rates, and precision' which are not mentioned in the source text, reducing the score. Supported aspects include: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment' and 'continuous synchronization with physical assets through sensor data'.

The extraction makes unsupported claims about specific validation mechanisms, such as 'automatically reconciling runtime sensor and actuator data' to 'detect mismatches'. The text mentions 'validating the model' only as a pre-deployment step and 'synchronization' as a live operation, but does not describe ongoing mismatch detection or fidelity assurance in the manner presented. Quotes: 'validating the model', 'Real-time data integration... enabling live synchronization'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

While the documents discuss a 'model-driven deployment framework' and 'scalable deployment', these refer to the software engineering process and architectural modularity (extension points) rather than specific hosting infrastructure details such as cloud/edge platforms, computational requirements, or storage. block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
